name: responsibility
best_quote: In the E-step, each observation is assigned a responsibility or weight for each cluster, based on the likelihood of each of the corresponding Gaussians.

name: model-free methods
best_quote: In this chapter we discuss some simple and essentially model-free methods for classification and pattern recognition. Because they are highly unstructured, they typically are not useful for understanding the nature of the relationship between the features and class outcome. However, as black box prediction engines, they can be very effective, and are often among the best performers in real data problems.

name: learning vector quantization
best_quote: In this technique due to Kohonen (1989), prototypes are placed strategically with respect to the decision boundaries in an ad-hoc way. LVQ is an online algorithm—observations are processed one at a time. The idea is that the training points attract prototypes of the correct class, and repel other prototypes.

name: soft clustering
best_quote: As a consequence, the Gaussian mixture model is often referred to as a soft clustering method, while K-means is hard.

name: hard clustering
best_quote: As a consequence, the Gaussian mixture model is often referred to as a soft clustering method, while K-means is hard.

name: soft classification
best_quote: Similarly, when Gaussian mixture models are used to represent the feature density in each class, it produces smooth posterior probabilities ˆp(x) = {ˆp1(x), . . . , ˆpK(x)} for classifying x (see (12.60) on page 449.) Often this is interpreted as a soft classification, while in fact the classification rule is ˆG(x) = arg maxk ˆpk(x).

name: Cover and Hart result
best_quote: A famous result of Cover and Hart (1967) shows that asymptotically the error rate of the 1-nearest-neighbor classifier is never more than twice the Bayes rate.

name: adaptive nearest-neighbor
best_quote: The adaptive nearest-neighbor rules, described later in this chapter, are an attempt to alleviate this bias.

name: Voronoi tessellation
best_quote: The straight lines show the partitioning of points, each sector being the set of points closest to each centroid. This partitioning is called the Voronoi tessellation.

name: codebook
best_quote: The clustering process is called the encoding step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.

name: decoding step
best_quote: The process of constructing the approximate image from the centroids is called the decoding step.

name: lossy compression
best_quote: What we have described is known as lossy compression, since our images are degraded versions of the original. The degradation or distortion is usually measured in terms of mean squared error.

name: chaining
best_quote: Single linkage (14.41) only requires that a single dissimilarity dii′, i ∈G and i′ ∈H, be small for two groups G and H to be considered close together, irrespective of the other observation dissimilarities between the groups. It will therefore have a tendency to combine, at relatively low thresholds, observations linked by a series of close intermediate observations. This phenomenon, referred to as chaining, is often considered a defect of the method.

name: left singular vectors
best_quote: Here U is an N × p orthogonal matrix (UT U = Ip) whose columns uj are called the left singular vectors

name: principal components
best_quote: The columns of UD are called the principal components of X (see Section 3.5.1). The N optimal λ̂i in (14.52) are given by the first q principal components (the N rows of the N × q matrix UqDq).

name: Procrustes distance
best_quote: and the minimal distances is referred to as the Procrustes distance.

name: principal points of the distribution
best_quote: The set of k points that minimize the expected distance from X to its prototype are called the principal points of the distribution. Each principal point is self-consistent, in that it equals the mean of X in its Voronoi region.

name: adjacency matrix
best_quote: The matrix of edge weights W = {wii′} from a similarity graph is called the adjacency matrix.

name: unnormalized graph Laplacian
best_quote: This is called the unnormalized graph Laplacian; a number of normalized versions have been proposed—these standardize the Laplacian with respect to the node degrees gi, for example, L̃ = I − G−1W.

name: factor loadings
best_quote: The columns of A are referred to as the factor loadings, and are used to name and interpret the factors.

name: Local MDS
best_quote: In contrast, local multidimensional scaling (right panel) does a good job of preserving the ordering of the points along the curve.

name: singular value decomposition
best_quote: This is a standard decomposition in numerical analysis, and many algorithms exist for its computation (Golub and Van Loan, 1983, for example). Here U is an N × p orthogonal matrix (UT U = Ip) whose columns uj are called the left singular vectors; V is a p×p orthogonal matrix (VT V = Ip) with columns vj called the right singular vectors, and D is a p×p diagonal matrix, with diagonal elements d1 ≥ d2 ≥ ⋅⋅⋅ ≥ dp ≥ 0 known as the singular values.

name: reproducing kernel hilbert space
best_quote: We can gain more insight into kernel PCA by viewing the zm as sample evaluations of principal component functions gm ∈ HK, with HK the reproducing kernel Hilbert space generated by K

name: prototype methods
best_quote: Prototype methods represent the training data by a set of points in feature space. These prototypes are typically not examples from the training sample, except in the case of 1-nearest-neighbor classification discussed later. Each prototype has an associated class label, and classification of a query point x is made to the class of the closest prototype.

name: k-means clustering for classification
best_quote: To use K-means clustering for classification of labeled data, the steps are: apply K-means clustering to the training data in each class separately, using R prototypes per class

name: within cluster variance
best_quote: One chooses the desired number of cluster centers, say R, and the K-means procedure iteratively moves the centers to minimize the total within cluster variance.

name: LVQ1
best_quote: The procedure just described is actually called LVQ1. Modifications (LVQ2, LVQ3, etc.) have been proposed, that can sometimes improve performance.

name: LVQ2
best_quote: Modifications (LVQ2, LVQ3, etc.) have been proposed, that can sometimes improve performance.

name: LVQ3
best_quote: Modifications (LVQ2, LVQ3, etc.) have been proposed, that can sometimes improve performance.

name: online algorithm
best_quote: LVQ is an online algorithm—observations are processed one at a time.

name: stochastic approximation learning rates
best_quote: The learning rate ϵ is decreased to zero with each iteration, following the guidelines for stochastic approximation learning rates (Section 11.4).

name: k-nearest-neighbor classifier
best_quote: These classifiers are memory-based, and require no model to be fit. Given a query point x0, we find the k training points x(r), r = 1, . . . , k closest in distance to x0, and then classify using majority vote among the k neighbors.

name: memory-based classifier
best_quote: These classifiers are memory-based, and require no model to be fit.

name: majority vote
best_quote: Given a query point x0, we find the k training points x(r), r = 1, . . . , k closest in distance to x0, and then classify using majority vote among the k neighbors.

name: asymptotic nearest-neighbor error bound
best_quote: For K = 2 the 1-nearest-neighbor error rate is 2pk∗(x)(1 −pk∗(x)) ≤2(1 −pk∗(x)) (twice the Bayes error rate).

name: discriminant adaptive nearest-neighbor
best_quote: Here we develop a locally adaptive form of nearest-neighbor classification that tries to use class information to adaptively shape the metric in each neighborhood. This is done by shaping the neighborhood locally to concentrate on the dimensions in which the information about the class probabilities is most likely to be

name: chi-square distance
best_quote: The chi-square distance is a natural choice for discrete distributions. If p = (p1, p2, . . . , pm) and q = (q1, q2, . . . , qm) are two discrete distributions, the chi-square distance is

name: market basket analysis
best_quote: Market basket analysis is the prototypical example of unsupervised learning. It is a method for finding relationships between items in large databases of customer transactions. The idea is to discover what items or products are purchased together.

name: association rules
best_quote: An association rule is an implication expression of the form X ⇒ Y, where X and Y are disjoint itemsets. The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y. The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.

name: support
best_quote: The rule X ⇒ Y has support s in the transaction set if s% of transactions contain X ∪ Y.

name: confidence
best_quote: The rule has confidence c if c% of transactions that contain X also contain Y.

name: apriori algorithm
best_quote: The Apriori algorithm is an efficient bottom-up algorithm for mining all itemsets that satisfy a minimum support threshold. It exploits the downward closure property: if an itemset is frequent, then all of its subsets must also be frequent.

name: downward closure property
best_quote: It exploits the downward closure property: if an itemset is frequent, then all of its subsets must also be frequent.

name: frequent itemset
best_quote: The Apriori algorithm is an efficient bottom-up algorithm for mining all itemsets that satisfy a minimum support threshold.

name: lift
best_quote: The lift of a rule is defined as the confidence divided by the support of Y (the baseline frequency): lift(X ⇒ Y) = conf(X ⇒ Y)/supp(Y).

name: generalized association rules
best_quote: Generalized association rules allow for quantitative and categorical variables. The basic strategy is to discretize these variables and then apply a standard association rule mining algorithm.

name: unsupervised as supervised learning
best_quote: Unsupervised learning problems can be recast as supervised learning problems. The idea is to create a two-class response: for each itemset, code each transaction as 1 if it contains the itemset, and 0 otherwise. Then use a supervised learning method to predict the response from the other features.

name: cluster analysis
best_quote: Cluster analysis, also called data segmentation, has a variety of goals. All relate to grouping or segmenting a collection of objects into subsets or clusters, such that those within each cluster are more closely related to one another than objects assigned to different clusters.

name: data segmentation
best_quote: Cluster analysis, also called data segmentation, has a variety of goals.

name: dissimilarity
best_quote: Most algorithms use dissimilarities d(xi, xi′) or similarities s(xi, xi′) between pairs of observations.

name: attribute dissimilarity
best_quote: For data with p quantitative features, Euclidean distance is the most common choice. Other choices include L1 (Manhattan) distance and Lq distance for general q.

name: gower dissimilarity
best_quote: A general dissimilarity coefficient due to Gower (1971) handles all attribute types. For each attribute, a dissimilarity dj(xij, xi′j) is computed, and these are combined into an overall dissimilarity.
formula: d(x_i, x_{i'}) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{ij} d_j(x_{ij}, x_{i'j})}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{ij}}

name: object dissimilarity
best_quote: In some applications the features are not used at all. Rather, the user supplies an N × N matrix of pairwise dissimilarities among the observations, based on domain knowledge.

name: combinatorial clustering algorithms
best_quote: Combinatorial algorithms work directly with the observed data or pairwise dissimilarities. They produce a partition of the data, assigning each observation to one and only one cluster.

name: mixture modeling clustering
best_quote: Mixture modeling assumes that the data are generated by a mixture of underlying probability distributions. Each component distribution represents a cluster.

name: mode seeking clustering
best_quote: Mode seeking ('bump hunting') methods take a nonparametric perspective, attempting to directly estimate distinct modes of the probability density function. Observations 'closest' to each respective mode then define the individual clusters.

name: hierarchical clustering
best_quote: Hierarchical clustering produces a nested sequence of partitions, from single observation clusters at the bottom to one cluster containing all observations at the top. A dendrogram represents this hierarchy.

name: dendrogram
best_quote: A dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format.

name: agglomerative clustering
best_quote: Agglomerative (bottom-up) clustering starts with each observation in its own cluster. The two closest clusters are merged, and this is repeated until there is only one cluster.

name: divisive clustering
best_quote: Divisive (top-down) clustering starts with all observations in one cluster. The cluster is split into two, and this is repeated recursively until each observation is in its own cluster.

name: single linkage
best_quote: Single linkage (nearest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity between two groups as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other: d_SL(G, H) = min_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
formula: d_{SL}(G, H) = \min_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

name: complete linkage
best_quote: Complete linkage (furthest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity as the maximum dissimilarity: d_CL(G, H) = max_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
formula: d_{CL}(G, H) = \max_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

name: average linkage
best_quote: Group average defines the dissimilarity as the average of all pairwise dissimilarities: d_GA(G, H) = (1/(N_G × N_H)) ∑_{i∈G} ∑_{i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
formula: d_{GA}(G, H) = \frac{1}{N_G N_H} \sum_{i \in G} \sum_{i' \in H} d_{ii'}

name: Ward's method
best_quote: Ward's method uses an ANOVA-based approach that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares. At each step, merge the pair of clusters that leads to the minimum increase in total within-cluster variance.

name: vector quantization
best_quote: The K-means clustering algorithm represents a key tool in the apparently unrelated area of image and signal compression, particularly in vector quantization or VQ

name: codeword
best_quote: Each of the 512 × 512 pixel blocks (or points) is approximated by its closest cluster centroid, known as a codeword.

name: encoding step
best_quote: The clustering process is called the encoding step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.

name: rate-distortion tradeoff
best_quote: More generally a rate/distortion curve would be used to assess the tradeoff.

name: variable length coding
best_quote: Using Shannon coding theory, we know that in general a variable length code will do better, and the rate then becomes −∑K ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4. The term in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓ of the codewords in the image.

name: entropy of codeword distribution
best_quote: The term in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓ of the codewords in the image.

name: tree-structured vector quantization
best_quote: For example, tree-structured VQ finds the centroids with a top-down, 2-means style algorithm, as alluded to in Section 14.3.12. This allows successive refinement of the compression.

name: k-medoids clustering
best_quote: K-medoids clustering is a variant of K-means where the cluster representatives are restricted to be observations in the dataset. For each cluster, find the observation that minimizes the total distance to other points in that cluster.

name: silhouette coefficient
best_quote: The silhouette coefficient s(i) for observation i is defined as s(i) = (b(i) - a(i))/max{a(i), b(i)}, where a(i) is the average dissimilarity of i to all other objects in the same cluster, and b(i) is the minimum average dissimilarity of i to objects in other clusters.

name: gap statistic
best_quote: The recently proposed Gap statistic (Tibshirani et al., 2001b) compares the curve log WK to the curve obtained from data uniformly distributed over a rectangle containing the data.

name: self-organizing map
best_quote: Self-organizing maps (SOMs) can be viewed as constrained versions of K-means clustering, in which the prototypes are encouraged to lie in a one- or two-dimensional manifold in the feature space. The original motivation was to produce low-dimensional views of high-dimensional data, in a way similar to principal components and multidimensional scaling.

name: principal curves
best_quote: Principal curves generalize the principal component line, providing a smooth one-dimensional curved approximation to a set of data points in IRp.

name: principal surfaces
best_quote: Principal surfaces have exactly the same form as principal curves, but are of higher dimension.

name: spectral clustering
best_quote: Spectral clustering finds the m eigenvectors Z_{N×m} corresponding to the m smallest eigenvalues of L (ignoring the trivial constant eigenvector). Using a standard method like K-means, we then cluster the rows of Z to yield a clustering of the original data points.

name: graph laplacian
best_quote: Finally, the graph Laplacian is defined by

name: similarity graph
best_quote: We represent the observations in an undirected similarity graph G = ⟨V, E⟩. The N vertices vi represent the observations

name: kernel density estimation
best_quote: Kernel density estimation is a general technique for estimating probability densities from data. A kernel function K_λ is centered at each observation, and the density estimate is the sum (or average) of these kernels.

name: encoding step in vector quantization
best_quote: The clustering process is called the encoding step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.

name: rate in bits per pixel
best_quote: This is typically expressed as a rate in bits per pixel: log2(K)/4, which are 1.91 and 0.50, respectively.

name: fixed-length code
best_quote: This uses a fixed-length code, and is inefficient if some codewords occur many more times than others in the image.

name: variable length code
best_quote: Using Shannon coding theory, we know that in general a variable length code will do better, and the rate then becomes −∑K ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4.

name: cophenetic correlation coefficient
best_quote: The extent to which the hierarchical structure produced by a dendrogram actually represents the data itself can be judged by the cophenetic correlation coefficient.

name: cophenetic dissimilarity
best_quote: The cophenetic dissimilarity Cii′ between two observations (i, i′) is the intergroup dissimilarity at which observations i and i′ are first joined together in the same cluster.

name: ultrametric inequality
best_quote: Also these dissimilarities obey the ultrametric inequality

name: single linkage agglomerative clustering
best_quote: Single linkage (SL) agglomerative clustering takes the intergroup dissimilarity to be that of the closest (least dissimilar) pair
formula: d_{SL}(G, H) = \min_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

name: complete linkage agglomerative clustering
best_quote: Complete linkage (CL) agglomerative clustering (furthest-neighbor technique) takes the intergroup dissimilarity to be that of the furthest (most dissimilar) pair
formula: d_{CL}(G, H) = \max_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

name: group average clustering
best_quote: Group average (GA) clustering uses the average dissimilarity between the groups
formula: d_{GA}(G, H) = \frac{1}{N_G N_H} \sum_{i \in G} \sum_{i' \in H} d_{ii'}

name: cluster diameter
best_quote: If we define the diameter DG of a group of observations as the largest dissimilarity among its members

name: batch som
best_quote: The original SOM algorithm was online—observations are processed one at a time—and later a batch version was proposed.

name: neighborhood function in som
best_quote: where the neighborhood function h gives more weight to prototypes mk with indices ℓk closer to ℓj than to those further away.

name: reconstruction error in clustering
best_quote: One can do this by computing the reconstruction error ||x − mj||², summed over observations, for both methods.

name: term-document matrix
best_quote: A term-document matrix is created, where each row represents a single document. The entries in each row are the relative frequency of each of a predefined set of terms.

name: principal component analysis
best_quote: The principal components of a set of data in IRp provide a sequence of best linear approximations to that data, of all ranks q ≤ p.

name: rank-q linear model
best_quote: consider the rank-q linear model for representing them

name: right singular vectors
best_quote: V is a p×p orthogonal matrix (VT V = Ip) with columns vj called the right singular vectors

name: singular values
best_quote: D is a p×p diagonal matrix, with diagonal elements d1 ≥ d2 ≥ ⋅⋅⋅ ≥ dp ≥ 0 known as the singular values.

name: procrustes transformation
best_quote: with X1 and X2 both N × p matrices of corresponding points, R an orthonormal p × p matrix, and μ a p-vector of location coordinates.

name: procrustes average
best_quote: Related to Procrustes distance is the Procrustes average of a collection of L shapes

name: principal curve
best_quote: This says f(λ) is the average of all data points that project to it, that is, the points for which it is 'responsible.' This is also known as a self-consistency property.

name: self-consistency property
best_quote: This says f(λ) is the average of all data points that project to it, that is, the points for which it is 'responsible.' This is also known as a self-consistency property.

name: principal surface
best_quote: Principal surfaces have exactly the same form as principal curves, but are of higher dimension.

name: mutual k-nearest-neighbor graph
best_quote: The most popular is the mutual K-nearest-neighbor graph.

name: normalized graph laplacian
best_quote: This is called the unnormalized graph Laplacian; a number of normalized versions have been proposed—these standardize the Laplacian with respect to the node degrees gi

name: kernel principal components
best_quote: Kernel PCA (Schölkopf et al., 1999) expand the scope of PCA, mimicking what we would obtain if we were to expand the features by non-linear transformations, and then apply PCA in this transformed feature space.

name: sparse principal components
best_quote: In this section we briefly discuss some methods for deriving principal components with sparse loadings. They are all based on lasso (L1) penalties.

name: scotlass
best_quote: The SCoTLASS procedure of Jolliffe et al. (2003) takes the first approach

name: elastic net for sparse pca
best_quote: Zou et al. (2006) start instead with the regression/reconstruction property of PCA, similar to the approach in Section 14.5.1.

name: non-negative matrix factorization
best_quote: Non-negative matrix factorization (Lee and Seung, 1999) is a recent alternative approach to principal components analysis, in which the data and components are assumed to be non-negative.

name: archetypal analysis
best_quote: This method, due to Cutler and Breiman (1994), approximates data points by prototypes that are themselves linear combinations of data points.

name: factor analysis
best_quote: The classical factor analysis model, developed primarily by researchers in psychometrics, alleviates these problems to some extent

name: independent component analysis
best_quote: The independent component analysis (ICA) model has exactly the same form as (14.78), except the Si are assumed to be statistically independent rather than uncorrelated.

name: blind source separation
best_quote: ICA is able to perform blind source separation, by exploiting the independence and non-Gaussianity of the original sources.

name: differential entropy
best_quote: The differential entropy H of a random variable Y with density g(y) is given by

name: mutual information
best_quote: Finally, the mutual information I(Y) between the components of the random vector Y is a natural measure of dependence

name: negentropy
best_quote: For convenience, rather than using the entropy H(Yj), Hyvärinen and Oja (2000) use the negentropy measure J(Yj) defined by

name: exploratory projection pursuit
best_quote: Friedman and Tukey (1974) proposed exploratory projection pursuit, a graphical exploration technique for visualizing high-dimensional data.

name: projection indices
best_quote: They proposed a number of projection indices for optimization, each focusing on a different departure from Gaussianity.

name: tilted gaussian density
best_quote: In the spirit of representing departures from Gaussianity, we represent each fj as fj(sj) = φ(sj)e^(gj(sj)), a tilted Gaussian density.

name: prodeniica
best_quote: Full details can be found in Hastie and Tibshirani (2003), and the method is implemented in the R package ProDenICA

name: amari metric
best_quote: We used the Amari metric (Bach and Jordan, 2002) as a measure of the closeness of the two frames

name: multidimensional scaling
best_quote: Multidimensional scaling seeks values z1, z2, . . . , zN ∈ R^k to minimize the so-called stress function

name: kruskal-shephard scaling
best_quote: This is known as least squares or Kruskal–Shephard scaling.

name: sammon mapping
best_quote: A variation on least squares scaling is the so-called Sammon mapping which minimizes

name: classical scaling
best_quote: If the similarities are in fact centered inner-products, classical scaling is exactly equivalent to principal components

name: metric scaling
best_quote: Least squares and classical scaling are referred to as metric scaling methods, in the sense that the actual dissimilarities or similarities are approximated.

name: shephard-kruskal nonmetric scaling
best_quote: Shephard–Kruskal nonmetric scaling effectively uses only ranks. Nonmetric scaling seeks to minimize the stress function

name: isotonic regression
best_quote: With the zi fixed, the method of isotonic regression is used to find the best monotonic approximation θ(dii′) to ||zi − zi′||.

name: isometric feature mapping
best_quote: Isometric feature mapping (ISOMAP) (Tenenbaum et al., 2000) constructs a graph to approximate the geodesic distance between points along the manifold.

name: local linear embedding
best_quote: Local linear embedding (Roweis and Saul, 2000) takes a very different approach, trying to preserve the local affine structure of the high-dimensional data.

name: geodesic distance on manifold
best_quote: The geodesic distance between any two points is then approximated by the shortest path between points on the graph.

name: pagerank algorithm
best_quote: The PageRank algorithm considers a webpage to be important if many other webpages point to it. However the linking webpages that point to a given page are not treated equally: the algorithm also takes into account both the importance (PageRank) of the linking pages and the number of outgoing links that they have.

name: proximity matrix
best_quote: In growing a random forest, an N × N proximity matrix is accumulated for the training data. For every tree, any pair of oob observations sharing a terminal node has their proximity increased by one. This proximity matrix is then represented in two dimensions using multidimensional scaling.

name: random forest
best_quote: Random forests (Breiman, 2001) is a substantial modification of bagging that builds a large collection of de-correlated trees, and then averages them. On many problems the performance of random forests is very similar to boosting, and they are simpler to train and tune.

name: out-of-bag samples
best_quote: For each observation zi = (xi, yi), construct its random forest predictor by averaging only those trees corresponding to bootstrap samples in which zi did not appear.

name: out-of-bag error
best_quote: An oob error estimate is almost identical to that obtained by N-fold cross-validation; see Exercise 15.2. Hence unlike many other nonlinear estimators, random forests can be fit in one sequence, with cross-validation being performed along the way.

name: variable importance via permutation
best_quote: When the bth tree is grown, the oob samples are passed down the tree, and the prediction accuracy is recorded. Then the values for the jth variable are randomly permuted in the oob samples, and the accuracy is again computed. The decrease in accuracy as a result of this permuting is averaged over all trees, and is used as a measure of the importance of variable j in the random forest.

name: proximity plot
best_quote: This proximity matrix is then represented in two dimensions using multidimensional scaling (Section 14.8). The idea is that even though the data may be high-dimensional, involving mixed variables, etc., the proximity plot gives an indication of which observations are effectively close together in the eyes of the random forest classifier.

name: de-correlation effect in random forests
best_quote: The idea in random forests (algorithm 15.1) is to improve the variance reduction of bagging by reducing the correlation between the trees, without increasing the variance too much. This is achieved in the tree-growing process through random selection of the input variables.

name: sampling correlation in random forests
best_quote: ρ(x) is the sampling correlation between any pair of trees used in the averaging: ρ(x) = corr[T(x; Θ1(Z)), T(x; Θ2(Z))], where Θ1(Z) and Θ2(Z) are a randomly drawn pair of random forest trees grown to the randomly sampled Z

name: within-z variance
best_quote: The second term is the within-Z variance— a result of the randomization, which increases as m decreases. The first term is in fact the sampling variance of the random forest ensemble (shown in the right panel), which decreases as m decreases.

name: random forest as adaptive nearest neighbors
best_quote: The random forest classifier has much in common with the k-nearest neighbor classifier (Section 13.3); in fact a weighted version thereof. Since each tree is grown to maximal size, for a particular Θ∗, T(x; Θ∗(Z)) is the response value for one of the training samples. The tree-growing algorithm finds an 'optimal' path to that observation, choosing the most informative predictors from those at its disposal.

name: forward stagewise linear regression
best_quote: At each successive step, the tree Tk∗ is selected that best fits the current residuals in line 2(a). Its corresponding coefficient α̌k∗ is then incremented or decremented by an infinitesimal amount in 2(b), while all other coefficients α̌k, k ≠ k∗ are left unchanged.

name: l1 normalized margin
best_quote: Schapire et al. (1998) define the normalized L1 margin of a fitted model f(x) = Σk αkTk(x) as min[yi·f(xi)]/Σk|αk|. Here the minimum is taken over the training sample, and yi ∈ {−1, +1}. Unlike the L2 margin (4.40) of support vector machines, the L1 margin m(f) measures the distance to the closest training point in L∞ units (maximum coordinate distance).

name: markov random field
best_quote: We restrict our discussion to undirected graphical models, also known as Markov random fields or Markov networks. In these graphs, the absence of an edge between two vertices has a special meaning: the corresponding random variables are conditionally independent, given the other variables.

name: pairwise markov independence
best_quote: In a Markov graph G, the absence of an edge implies that the corresponding random variables are conditionally independent given the variables at the other vertices. This is expressed with the following notation: X ⊥ Y | rest if X and Y are not adjacent, where 'rest' refers to all of the other vertices in the graph.

name: clique potential
best_quote: A probability density function f over a Markov graph G can be can represented as [product over cliques] where C is the set of maximal cliques, and the positive functions ψC(·) are called clique potentials. These are not in general density functions, but rather are affinities that capture the dependence in XC by scoring certain instances xC higher than others.

name: hammersley-clifford theorem
best_quote: This result holds for Markov networks G with positive distributions, and is known as the Hammersley-Clifford theorem (Hammersley and Clifford, 1971; Clifford, 1990).
